task:
[Read from: /home/spencer/Repositories/predictability-aware-training/plan.md, /home/spencer/Repositories/predictability-aware-training/progress.md]

RED-TEAM the experimental design and scientific claims in /home/spencer/Repositories/predictability-aware-training — read RESEARCH.md, PLAN.md, RESULTS.md, then model.py/train.py as needed. The project claims: folding expert-prediction accuracy into an MoE's training loss makes the model more predictable (higher expert hit@k) at zero quality cost, enabling disk-expert prefetching. Attack the methodology: (1) Confounds — is the posthoc control fairly matched (predictor capacity, training steps 2000 vs 6000, same data stream position)? (2) Metric validity — is hit@k (top-k set overlap) the right proxy for prefetch success, or should it be e.g. weighted by expert size, per-token cache-miss cost, or recall@k under a cache budget? (3) The 'zero quality cost' claim — is val LM loss on a continuation of the training stream a valid quality measure, or is it just train loss by another name? What would contamination do to the claim? (4) Statistical rigor — single seed, eval variance across stream chunks; are +2.5pt differences meaningful? (5) Does the λ=0.03≈posthoc observation actually isolate 'training-induced predictability' as claimed? Alternative explanations? (6) Threats to the end-to-end thesis: even with perfect prediction, does prefetching actually pay at 2MB expert size and modern NVMe latency vs. just caching hot experts (Colibri's learned hot-store)? When would prediction NOT help? (7) Scale threats: why might Tier B (8 experts, 94M) results fail to transfer to 256-expert production MoEs? READ-ONLY: do not modify files, do not run training. Return a numbered list of the strongest attacks, each with severity and a concrete experimental fix or additional measurement that would address it.

criteria.must: Return concrete findings with file paths and severity when applicable
Return concrete findings with file paths and severity when applicable

criteriaSatisfied.evidence: Returned 10 numbered red-team findings with severities and concrete fixes, citing train.py:21,37,72,78-81,96, model.py:104-106,122-141,159,163-164, and log evidence from run_tierB_base.log, run_h124.log, run_h124_rest.log, run_tierB_posthoc.log, run_lambda.log (e.g., identical baseline scoring 5.942 vs 6.285 nats across experiments; h4 hit@k swinging 0.802→0.760 within one run).

commandsRun.command: grep/sed inspection of run_*.log, run_*.sh, train.py, model.py (read-only)
commandsRun.summary: Verified eval values, run configurations, posthoc control setup, and metric implementation against RESULTS.md claims

validationOutput: run_tierB_base.log final eval lm 5.942 vs run_h124_rest.log baseline final eval lm 6.285 (same seed/architecture) — eval chunk variance 0.34 nats vs claimed quality deltas of 0.005-0.014 nats
run_h124.log joint h4 hit@k 0.802@step2000/4500 → 0.760@step6000 — 4.2pt eval jitter vs +2.5pt claimed treatment effects
run_tierB_posthoc.log: posthoc control = 2000 steps, linear head, fresh stream restart at offset 0; joint runs = 6000 steps at ~24.6M token offset
residualRisks: Backbone-predictability ablation (posthoc-on-joint-backbone) is necessary but the accuracy bar is the co-trained predictor's number, which may depend on 6000 steps of joint history
Economic case for prefetching remains unproven until the planned cache simulation and pread benchmark (PLAN.md step 5) exist
Read-only review: no fixes applied; all findings require new training/eval runs by the owner
childReport.diffSummary: No diff — read-only review, no files modified
reviewFindings: critical: RESULTS.md Exp1-3 - 'zero quality cost' deltas (0.005-0.014 nats) are ~2 orders of magnitude below the eval protocol's noise floor (0.34-nat spread for identical baselines, 5-batch evals from the training stream, train.py:37,96)

critical: RESULTS.md Exp3 - 'genuine training-induced predictability' claim never isolates backbone predictability from predictor co-adaptation; missing posthoc-on-frozen-joint-backbone ablation (trainable today via train.py --mode posthoc --ckpt ckpt_lam0.3_joint.pt)
high: model.py:159 - degenerate predictability (static/copied routing) not ruled out; no router entropy, utilization, or cross-layer routing-MI measurements anywhere in RESULTS.md
high: model.py:163-164 - hit@k is not the prefetch objective; no cache-budget recall, no misprefetch bandwidth penalty, k_pred fixed = top_k, cache simulation (PLAN.md step 5) unbuilt
high: train.py:78-81 + run_tierB_posthoc.log - posthoc 'SOTA control' undermatched: linear head vs cited 2-layer ranking-loss SOTA, 2000 vs 6000 steps, eval at stream offset ~8.4M vs ~24.6M tokens
high: train.py:21,37 - 'val LM loss' is the training objective on the train split; no held-out corpus or downstream probe; evals consume the training stream, desyncing cross-experiment data identity
medium-high: train.py:72 - single seed, no CIs; final-eval comparison chosen after observing mid-run fluctuation (acknowledged in RESULTS.md Exp2)

medium-high: end-to-end thesis untested - Tier B (94M params, ~1.6MB experts) is never disk-resident; prediction loses to caching when hot set fits budget, at prefill, and when bandwidth-bound; no tok/s evidence exists

medium: scale transfer - 8 experts/8 layers/25M tokens (75x undertrained) vs 256 experts/75 layers production; baseline hit@k 0.847 here vs Colibri's 0.716 on a real model suggests the toy regime is unrepresentative
low: RESULTS.md - Exp1 vs Exp2 tables present same-model evals on different stream chunks (5.942 vs 6.285) without flagging chunk variance, inviting misreading
childReport.manualNotes: The single highest-value action is attack #2's ablation: it requires no new backbone training (train.py already supports --mode posthoc --ckpt), directly tests the paper's central claim, and its outcome determines whether attacks #3-#5 even matter. Second priority is the fixed held-out eval set, which is a prerequisite for trusting any number in RESULTS.md.
